variables.quote: sick children age 2 months up to 5 years
date
CHW name
child's name
age (years and months)
sex
caregiver's name
relationship (mother/father/other)
address
LOOK for signs of illness
ASK the caregiver about the child's problems
Does the child have a cough?
If yes, for how long? A cough for 14 days or more is a danger sign.
Does the child have fever now or in the last 3 days?
If yes, when did it start?
Fever for 7 days or more is a danger sign.
Identify fever by the caregiver's report or by feeling the child's stomach or underarm.
Diarrhoea is 3 or more loose or watery stools in 24 hours.
Ask: For how long? ... Diarrhoea for 14 days or more is a danger sign.
Is there blood in the stool? ... Blood in stool (dysentery) is a danger sign.
A convulsion during the current illness is a danger sign. The child needs urgent referral.
A child who cannot drink or feed anything at all has a danger sign. Ask the caregiver to offer the child a drink or breastfeed.
A child who vomits everything and cannot hold anything down has a danger sign. A child who vomits sometimes but can hold down some fluids does not have this danger sign.
A child who is unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or unconscious has a danger sign. Try to wake the child by clapping or speaking loudly.
Look at the lower chest wall when the child breathes IN. Chest indrawing is when the lower chest wall goes in. The child must be calm; do not assess while crying or breastfeeding.
Count the child's breaths for one full minute while the child is calm and quiet.
2 months up to 12 months: 50 breaths per minute or more
12 months up to 5 years: 40 breaths per minute or more
Use the MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) strap on children age 6 months up to 5 years. Wrap the strap around the child's left upper arm, midway between shoulder and elbow.
GREEN: not malnourished
YELLOW: moderate malnutrition (refer to supplementary feeding if available)
RED: severe malnutrition - DANGER SIGN - refer urgently
Also check for swelling of both feet (oedema). Press thumbs gently on the top of each foot for 3 seconds. If dents remain on BOTH feet, the child has severe malnutrition.

Check expiry date; do not use expired tests. Wear new gloves for each child. Clean the child's ring finger with a spirit swab; let dry. Prick the finger with a lancet; collect blood with the loop. Place blood in square hole A on the test strip. Add 6 drops of buffer into round hole B. Wait 15 minutes.
POSITIVE = red lines in both C and T windows.
NEGATIVE = red line in C window only.
INVALID = no line in C window; repeat with new test.
Do a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) for malaria before treating. Do not give antimalarial medicine unless the RDT is positive.
2 months up to 6 months: 1/2 tablet once daily for 10 days (5 tablets)
6 months up to 5 years: 1 tablet once daily for 10 days (10 tablets)
Wash hands with soap and water. Pour entire ORS packet into a clean container. Add 1 litre of clean water. Stir until dissolved.
2 months up to 12 months: 1 tablet twice daily for 5 days (10 tablets); 12 months up to 5 years: 2 tablets twice daily for 5 days (20 tablets)
Age Dose Frequency Duration/Supply: 2 months up to 3 years: 1 tablet AL, twice daily, 3 days/6 tablets; 3 years up to 5 years: 2 tablets AL, twice daily, 3 days/12 tablets
Give more fluids and continue feeding.
Return immediately if the child: cannot drink or feed, becomes sicker, or has blood in stool.
Follow up ALL children treated at home in 3 days. If the child is not better or is worse at follow-up, refer.
After 3 days: Is the child still having diarrhoea? If yes and for less than 14 days, continue ORS and zinc. If 14 days or more, refer.
After 3 days: Does the child still have fever? If RDT was positive and antimalarial was given and still has fever, refer.
After 3 days: Is the child still coughing? Count breaths again. If still fast breathing, refer. If breathing normal, complete amoxicillin course.
Cough with fast breathing (pneumonia, no chest indrawing)
Write a referral note including: child's name, age, date, reason for referral, danger signs found, treatments given.
If fever, keep the child cool during travel. If diarrhoea, give ORS solution during travel.
Follow up the child after return, at least once a week until well.
Malaria RDT, MUAC straps, timer or watch with second hand (for counting breaths), thermometer (optional), medicines and supplies.

predicates.quote: assess, classify, and treat sick children age 2 months up to 5 years in the community
A cough for 14 days or more is a danger sign.
Fever for 7 days or more is a danger sign.
Diarrhoea is 3 or more loose or watery stools in 24 hours.
Diarrhoea for 14 days or more is a danger sign.
Blood in stool (dysentery) is a danger sign.
A convulsion during the current illness is a danger sign.
A child who cannot drink or feed anything at all has a danger sign.
A child who vomits everything and cannot hold anything down has a danger sign.
A child who vomits sometimes but can hold down some fluids does not have this sign.
A child who is unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or unconscious has a danger sign.

YELLOW: moderate malnutrition (refer to supplementary feeding if available)
RED: severe malnutrition - DANGER SIGN - refer urgently

NEGATIVE = red line in C window only.
INVALID = no line in C window; repeat with new test.
Wait 15 minutes.
Add 6 drops of buffer into round hole B.
Store mixed ORS in a covered container; discard after 24 hours.
If the child vomits, wait 10 minutes, then give more slowly.
Give at least half a cup after each loose stool.
2 months up to 6 months 1/2 tablet Once daily 10 days / 5 tablets
6 months up to 5 years 1 tablet Once daily 10 days / 10 tablets
2 months up to 12 months 1 tablet Twice daily 5 days / 10 tablets
12 months up to 5 years 2 tablets Twice daily 5 days / 20 tablets
Follow up ALL children treated at home in 3 days. If the child is not better or is worse at follow-u
If yes and for less than 14 days, continue ORS and zinc. If 14 days or more, refer.
If RDT was positive and antimalarial was given, the child should improve. If still febrile, refer.
If RDT was negative and fever persists, refer.
Does the child have fever now or in the last 3 days?
Fever (less than 7 days) in a malaria area
Do not give antimalarial medicine unless the RDT is positive.
2 months up to 3 years 1 tablet AL Twice daily 3 days / 6 tablets
3 years up to 5 years 2 tablets AL Twice daily 3 days / 12 tablets
Give first dose immediately. Second dose 8 hours later.
Then morning and evening for 2 more days.
If the child spits out the entire dose, give another.
If unable to take medicine, refer to health facility.
Follow up in 3 days. If fever persists at follow-up, refer to health facility.
After 3 days: Is the child still coughing? Count breaths again. If still fast breathing, refer.
If breathing is normal and child is improving, complete the full 5-day course of amoxicillin.
Give the first dose of any appropriate medicine before referral.
If fever, keep the child cool during travel.
If diarrhoea, give ORS solution during travel.
Follow up the child after return, at least once a week until well.

router.quote: A cough for 14 days or more is a danger sign.
Fever for 7 days or more is a danger sign.
Diarrhoea for 14 days or more is a danger sign.
Blood in stool (dysentery) is a danger sign.
A convulsion during the current illness is a danger sign. The child needs urgent referral.
A child who vomits everything and cannot hold anything down has a danger sign.
A child who cannot drink or feed anything at all has a danger sign.
A child who is unusually sleepy, difficult to wake, or unconscious has a danger sign.
Chest indrawing is when the lower chest wall goes IN when the child breathes in. This is a danger si
2 months up to 12 months 50 breaths per minute or more
12 months up to 5 years 40 breaths per minute or more
RED: severe malnutrition - DANGER SIGN - refer urgently
YELLOW: moderate malnutrition (refer to supplementary feeding if available)
GREEN: not malnourished
If dents remain on BOTH feet, the child has severe malnutrition. Refer urgently.
CHWs identify signs of illness, treat diarrhoea, confirmed malaria, and pneumonia (cough with fast breathing) at home
urgently refer children with danger signs to a health facility.
The findings determine whether to refer or treat at home.
Do a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) for malaria before treating. Do not give antimalarial medicine unle
POSITIVE = red lines in both C and T windows.
If RDT is negative: do not give antimalarial. Follow up in 3 days. If fever persists at follow-up, r
INVALID = no line in C window; repeat with new test.
Give first dose immediately. Second dose 8 hours later. Then morning and evening for 2 more days.
Follow up ALL children treated at home in 3 days. If the child is not better or is worse at follow-u
After 3 days: Is the child still having diarrhoea? If yes and for less than 14 days, continue ORS and zinc. If 14 days or more, refer.
After 3 days: Does the child still have fever? If RDT was positive and antimalarial was given, the child should improve. If still febrile, refer.
If RDT was negative and fever persists, refer.
After 3 days: Is the child still coughing? Count breaths again. If still fast breathing, refer.
If breathing is normal and child is improving, complete the full 5-day course of amoxicillin.
Return immediately if the child: cannot drink or feed, becomes sicker, or has blood in stool.
If fever, keep the child cool during travel.
If diarrhoea, give ORS solution during travel.
If unable to take medicine, refer to health facility.
